decisions.applies_to: All transitions from untrusted bytes through authenticated facts, commit plans, and accepted journals.
decisions.problem: A mutable object with verification booleans would let callers confuse inert data with authority and skip required stages.
decisions.selected_because: Distinct private-field stage types make invalid authority transitions harder to represent and keep verifier construction boundaries reviewable.
alternatives_considered: One transition object carrying is_valid, is_verified, and is_committed flags.
Caller-provided booleans returned by proof and signature adapters.
No additional pattern; keep one inert data type and explicit verifier functions.
tradeoffs: More intermediate types and explicit conversions must be documented and tested.
Versioned stage changes can require coordinated API updates.
review_triggers: A new boolean field or parameter represents verification, validity, authorization, commitment, acceptance, or trust.
A caller gains a public constructor for a sealed authority stage.

decisions.applies_to: Core dependencies and future database, proof, network, clock, randomness, and application integrations.
decisions.problem: Infrastructure dependencies in the semantic core would make accepted behavior platform-dependent and couple policy to one backend.
decisions.selected_because: Core-owned ports preserve inward dependency direction while adapters can vary without weakening reference semantics.
alternatives_considered: A central application service importing database, network, proof, and policy implementations.
Backend types exposed directly through semantic-core public APIs.
No additional pattern; keep direct dependencies while the core has no external boundary.
tradeoffs: Each adapter requires explicit conformance and failure-equivalence tests.
The workspace dependency allowlist must be updated whenever a reviewed crate is added.
review_triggers: A new crate or feature creates an edge not represented in the reviewed dependency policy.
An adapter needs time, environment, network, filesystem, randomness, or process access.

decisions.applies_to: Identifiers, roots, units, quantities, policies, verified facts, plans, and journals with enforced invariants.
decisions.problem: Primitive bytes, strings, public fields, or Default construction can bypass nonzero, domain, bound, and authority invariants.
decisions.selected_because: Opaque value objects with rejecting constructors centralize invariants and separate wire candidates from validated values.
alternatives_considered: Public protocol structs with caller-populated primitive fields.
Type aliases for IDs, roots, modes, units, and error codes.
No additional pattern; validate primitives at every use site.
tradeoffs: Callers must handle typed construction errors.
Wire and validated forms require deliberate conversion code.
review_triggers: An authority-relevant value gains public fields, Default, a string representation, or a raw byte alias.
Deserialization can construct a capability or validated plan directly.

decisions.applies_to: Normative authority bytes, hashes, list framing, domain separation, and strict decoders.
decisions.problem: General-purpose serialization can change defaults, ordering, enum tags, or trailing-byte behavior outside protocol review.
decisions.selected_because: A manual versioned codec exposes exact field order and bounds and can be replayed by an independent implementation.
alternatives_considered: Derive-based Serde or framework serialization as the authority hash source.
Diagnostic JSON reused as canonical protocol bytes.
No additional pattern; leave objects without an authority byte representation.
tradeoffs: Each schema needs explicit encode, decode, vector, mutation, and compatibility maintenance.
Canonical field changes require an RFC and versioned migration plan.
review_triggers: A new authority object gains bytes or hashes.
Field order, tags, domains, bounds, rejection precedence, or independent vectors change.

decisions.applies_to: State views, commits, verifier dispatch, hash suites, persistence, and application adapter interfaces.
decisions.problem: Manager traits and broad service objects combine unrelated authority and make substitution or reject-is-no-op behavior difficult to audit.
decisions.selected_because: Small core-owned capability ports make preconditions, side effects, error semantics, and replacement-adapter obligations explicit.
alternatives_considered: One manager interface for reads, writes, verification, networking, and reporting.
Application-owned callbacks returning booleans or untyped maps.
No additional pattern; use concrete functions until a genuine substitution boundary exists.
tradeoffs: Composition requires more explicit wiring between focused capabilities.
Interface changes must preserve Liskov-equivalent failure and binding semantics.
review_triggers: A public trait crosses the method review budget or combines read and commit authority.
A type named Manager, Handler, Processor, Utils, or Helpers enters core source.
